revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine
宇治上神社 | image = Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' main hall of the Ujigami Shrine,
a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]]

access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia0]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia24]]

https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia42‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia24]] [[Category:dd]]

Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. {{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine

a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]‎]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:qq]] [[category:ファイル]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine
宇治上神社 | image = Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' main hall of the Ujigami Shrine,
a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]‎]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]]

{{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine
宇治上神社 | image = Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' main hall of the Ujigami Shrine,
a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]‎]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]]

The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]‎]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]]

The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]]

Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]]

{{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine

a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:kk]]

The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. {{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine
宇治上神社 | image = Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' main hall of the Ujigami Shrine,
a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. {{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine

a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]

[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine
宇治上神社 | image = Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' main hall of the Ujigami Shrine,
a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ujigami Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]
