https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] }} [[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine
宇治上神社 | image = Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' main hall of the Ujigami Shrine,
a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] }} [[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]]

The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] }} [[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]]

[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] }} [[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]]

a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] }} [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]] [[en:Ujigami Shrine]]

[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine
宇治上神社 | image = Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' main hall of the Ujigami Shrine,
a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]] [[en:Ujigami Shrine]]

[[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]] [[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} [[cs:Svatyně Udžigami]] [[de:Ujigami-Schrein]] [[es:Santuario Ujigami]] [[eu:Ujigami santutegia]] [[fa:معبد اوجیگامی]] [[fi:Ujigami-jinja]] [[fr:Ujigami-jinja]] [[hy:Ուձիգամի]] [[ja:宇治上神社]] [[ko:우지가미 신사]] [[la:Templum Ujigami]] [[pl:Ujigami-jinja]] [[pt:Santuário de Ujigami]] [[ro:Ujigami (sanctuar)]] [[ru:Удзигами]] [[zh:宇治上神社]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine
宇治上神社 | image = Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' main hall of the Ujigami Shrine,
a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[:en:Dendrochronology|Dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] }} [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]] [[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} [[cs:Svatyně Udžigami]] [[de:Ujigami-Schrein]] [[es:Santuario Ujigami]] [[eu:Ujigami santutegia]] [[fa:معبد اوجیگامی]] [[fi:Ujigami-jinja]] [[fr:Ujigami-jinja]] [[hy:Ուձիգամի]] [[ja:宇治上神社]] [[ko:우지가미 신사]] [[la:Templum Ujigami]] [[pl:Ujigami-jinja]] [[pt:Santuário de Ujigami]] [[ro:Ujigami (sanctuar)]] [[ru:Удзигами]] [[zh:宇治上神社]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine
宇治上神社 | image = Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' main hall of the Ujigami Shrine,
a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[:en:Dendrochronology|Dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[:en:Dendrochronology|Dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] }} [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]] [[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} [[cs:Svatyně Udžigami]] [[de:Ujigami-Schrein]] [[es:Santuario Ujigami]] [[eu:Ujigami santutegia]] [[fa:معبد اوجیگامی]] [[fi:Ujigami-jinja]] [[fr:Ujigami-jinja]] [[hy:Ուձիգամի]] [[ja:宇治上神社]] [[ko:우지가미 신사]] [[la:Templum Ujigami]] [[pl:Ujigami-jinja]] [[pt:Santuário de Ujigami]] [[ro:Ujigami (sanctuar)]] [[ru:Удзигами]] [[zh:宇治上神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine
宇治上神社 | image = Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' main hall of the Ujigami Shrine,
a [[National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[Uji, Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[Uji, Kyoto|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in the [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[:en:Dendrochronology|Dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[:en:Dendrochronology|Dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] }} [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]] [[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} [[cs:Svatyně Udžigami]] [[de:Ujigami-Schrein]] [[es:Santuario Ujigami]] [[eu:Ujigami santutegia]] [[fa:معبد اوجیگامی]] [[fi:Ujigami-jinja]] [[fr:Ujigami-jinja]] [[hy:Ուձիգամի]] [[ja:宇治上神社]] [[ko:우지가미 신사]] [[la:Templum Ujigami]] [[pl:Ujigami-jinja]] [[pt:Santuário de Ujigami]] [[ro:Ujigami (sanctuar)]] [[ru:Удзигами]] [[zh:宇治上神社]]

The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] }} [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]] [[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} [[cs:Svatyně Udžigami]] [[de:Ujigami-Schrein]] [[es:Santuario Ujigami]] [[eu:Ujigami santutegia]] [[fa:معبد اوجیگامی]] [[fi:Ujigami-jinja]] [[fr:Ujigami-jinja]] [[hy:Ուձիգամի]] [[ja:宇治上神社]] [[ko:우지가미 신사]] [[la:Templum Ujigami]] [[pl:Ujigami-jinja]] [[pt:Santuário de Ujigami]] [[ro:Ujigami (sanctuar)]] [[ru:Удзигами]] [[zh:宇治上神社]]

{{infobox religious building | name = Ujigami Shrine
宇治上神社 | image = Ujigami jinja08s3s4500.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' main hall of the Ujigami Shrine,
a [[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] in the [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[:en:Dendrochronology|Dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} ‎]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] }} [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]] [[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} [[cs:Svatyně Udžigami]] [[de:Ujigami-Schrein]] [[es:Santuario Ujigami]] [[eu:Ujigami santutegia]] [[fa:معبد اوجیگامی]] [[fi:Ujigami-jinja]] [[fr:Ujigami-jinja]] [[hy:Ուձիգամի]] [[ja:宇治上神社]] [[ko:우지가미 신사]] [[la:Templum Ujigami]] [[pl:Ujigami-jinja]] [[pt:Santuário de Ujigami]] [[ro:Ujigami (sanctuar)]] [[ru:Удзигами]] [[zh:宇治上神社]]

a [[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|31|N|135|48|41|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Minor ''shikinaisha'' | deity = [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]]
[[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] | founded_by = | established = 11th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Ujiyamada, [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Ujigami Shrine'''|宇治上神社|Ujigami-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the city of [[:en:Uji|Uji]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine was built as a guardian shrine for the nearby [[Byōdō-in]], and is adjacent to the [[Uji Shrine]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] as one of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]". The ''[[honden]]'' and ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' have been designated by the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] as [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] in the [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|Category shrines]].{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-12-15 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2005-12-28 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}{{cite web | url = http://www.culturalprofiles.net/japan/units/4552.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title = Ujigami Shrine | year = 1996 | publisher = Kansai Digital Archives | location = Kita-ku, Osaka, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | archive-date = 2012-02-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120211232140/http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | url-status = dead }}{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = Ujigami-jinja | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-05-28 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }} The Ujigami Shrine is dedicated to the [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and his sons, the imperial princes [[Uji no Wakiiratsuko]] and [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]]. Uji no Wakiiratsuko committed suicide to solve a dispute over the imperial succession, and the shrine was built in his honor.{{cite web | url = http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | title = Ujigami-jinja Shrine | publisher = Kyoto Convention Bureau | location = Kyoto, Japan | access-date = 2012-05-28 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120523152426/http://www.kyoto.travel/2009/11/ujigami-jinja-shrine.html | archive-date = 2012-05-23 }} The [[honden]] of the Ujigami Shrine is known as the oldest example of ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style of shrine architecture in Japan. In this style of architecture the three inner shrine structures are built side-by-side, with the structure in the middle being larger than those to the left and right. The honden dates to the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794 – 1185). The haiden is built in the ''shinden-zukuri'' style, and its roof in the ''sugaruhafu'' style. The haiden dates to the Kamakura period (1185 – 1333). The Kasuga Shrine, also within the shrine precinct, dates to the same period. The shrine is noted for its freshwater spring. Ujigami Shrine was found via digital [[:en:Dendrochronology|Dendrochronology]] to be the oldest original [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Japan. The [[Nara Research Institute for Cultural Properties]] determined that the shrine was built in approximately 1060, which closely matches the written account of the founding of the shrine.{{cite web | url = http://repository.nabunken.go.jp/modules/xoonips/download.php?file_id=335 | script-title = ja:埋蔵文化財センター 古環境研究室 | publisher = Research Information Repository, Nara Cultural Properties Research Institute | location = Nara, Nara Prefecture, Japan | language = ja | access-date = 2012-05-28 }}{{Dead link|date=July 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=no }} Until the Meiji Period (1868 – 1912) the Uji and Ujigami shrines were collectively known as the Rikyukamisha. The annual festival of the Ujigami Shrine is held on May 5. [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine]] ==See also== *[[Uji Shrine]] *[[Ujigami]] *[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]] *[[Byōdō-in]] ==References== {{Commons Category|Ujigami Jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} ‎]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Uji, Kyoto]] }} [[sn:Ujigami Shrine]] [[en:Ujigami Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ujigami Jinja}} [[cs:Svatyně Udžigami]] [[de:Ujigami-Schrein]] [[es:Santuario Ujigami]] [[eu:Ujigami santutegia]] [[fa:معبد اوجیگامی]] [[fi:Ujigami-jinja]] [[fr:Ujigami-jinja]] [[hy:Ուձիգամի]] [[ja:宇治上神社]] [[ko:우지가미 신사]] [[la:Templum Ujigami]] [[pl:Ujigami-jinja]] [[pt:Santuário de Ujigami]] [[ro:Ujigami (sanctuar)]] [[ru:Удзигами]] [[zh:宇治上神社]]

Shrine | year = 2006 | publisher = Japan Cultural Profile | location = Cambridge, United Kingdom | access-date = 2012-05-28}}{{cite web | url = http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/uji/uji02-e.html | title
